title: Chapter 57
content:
Chapter 57: Sage Mode – On Medieval Architecture and Morning Blowjob, Round Two.

Clang, clang, clang.

The morning bell rang out from directly above. Rafilia-san was probably ringing it from the bell tower deep inside the cathedral.

The chickens we kept in the garden had also woken up and started crowing.

I shivered slightly from the chilly morning air that stung my skin.

It wasn’t that this room was built particularly sloppily—most buildings were like this—but the brick walls had wooden doors fitted into them with a gap of several centimeters at the bottom. It wasn’t for any particular reason; it was simply because architectural techniques were more primitive than in modern times. As expected of a northern city, the draft coming through the gap in the morning was quite cold.

The hinges (the metal fittings connecting the door to the wall) were on the inside, and the door opened inward from the room’s perspective.

Occasionally in fantasy manga or web novel adaptations, you’ll see doors that open outward when viewed from inside, but that’s incorrect. If a door opens outward, the hinges would structurally have to be on the outside as well. That would mean someone could just destroy the hinges and remove the door, allowing intruders to enter from outside.

In an era with poor public safety where there were no streetlights and thieves roamed at night, there was no way outward-opening doors would be built. There might be exceptions if you searched the entire world, but doors on buildings from the Middle Ages that still remain today are almost all inward-opening.

Thinking about architecture made me realize something else.

The other day, I inferred from the vegetation that Iria’s territory was in the northern region. The basis was that coniferous trees grow in high-latitude areas where the sun is lower.

I also examined the peculiarity of how the fortress city of Monvahn was built on wasteland despite the lack of nearby building materials.

Now that I think about it, there was something even more abnormal. Even if they transported building materials from distant places, why didn’t they use coniferous trees to build houses?

Imagine the historical cityscapes of medieval Europe. You’ll probably picture two main types: wooden multi-story buildings (4 floors or higher) or stone/brick low-rise buildings (3 floors or lower).

If you imagine a cityscape of four-story wooden buildings, it would resemble Rouen in northern France or Bremen and Hamburg in northern Germany. These cities are at high latitudes and had coniferous forests nearby, allowing them to build houses using straight, long timber.

Others might picture cityscapes with stone or brick low-rise buildings. Those would be southern cities like Marseille or Munich, where tall trees don’t grow. The abundance of stone buildings is because lightweight volcanic stone (tuff) that could be used as building material was plentiful nearby.

As these examples show, cityscapes weren’t determined by the vague category of "medieval Europe," but by the surrounding terrain. First, climate and latitude that determine vegetation. Next, the distribution of lightweight stone suitable for construction, which is closely tied to the location of volcanoes.

Since Monvahn is in the northern region, there should be coniferous forests within a few hours’ ride by carriage. Therefore, it wouldn’t be strange for it to have rows of wooden multi-story buildings. Even so, the highest they could build would be four stories, since they lacked the technology to build taller than wooden structures.

However, despite being in the north, Monvahn has almost no wooden multi-story buildings. Instead, there are mostly two- or three-story brick or stone buildings. This could be one of the pieces of evidence that Monvahn was built as a defensive stronghold. As the fairy tale "The Three Little Pigs" shows, stone and brick buildings are far stronger than wooden ones.

And until recently, I didn’t know how they transported the bricks used as building material.

But now I have one hypothesis.

Beastman slaves.

They might be turning female beastmen into sex slaves while using male beastmen as labor. From the filthy man who tried to sell a kemololi as edible meat, it’s clear that beastmen either breed easily or can be captured somewhere. If they were rare, there’s no way they’d be turned into food. Despite how common beastmen should be in this world, I hardly ever see them. I haven’t seen a single male beastman. They’re probably being forced into labor somewhere.

The reason I was pondering the distorted state of affairs surrounding Monvahn right after waking up was obvious.
